text:
Artificial intelligence is changing the digital world in fast pace. Researchers say that within five years more than 90% of the content that is generated will be generated using AI and human elements. In this synthetic society, it will become almost impossible to work out what is real and what is fake.

Regulating the Synthetic Society focuses on four advanced key technologies such as androids, deepfakes, augmented reality (AR) and virtual reality (VR) which are bearing on our world the characteristics of a synthetic society and are therefore subject to the regulation strategies introduced in this book. The four technologies mentioned are in a development phase and are capable of producing (already now!) original content indistinguishable from the real content provided by nature. Their possible applications have tremendous impact not only on democracy, on the judicial system and on the press, but even on our concept of relationship and therefore of family and on the social texture as a whole.

By Bart van der Sloot This accessible, in-depth book gives an unprecedented insight into the technology behind these new trends, mapping their architectures, uses and applications. It also looks at the potential benefits of these developments and considers the ways in which they could be used for fraudulent or criminal purposes, including identity theft and fraud, and the social evil of the sex trade. Finally, it considers the broader societal implications of these trends, including the promotion of post-truth, the commercialisation of the public domain, and possible risks to individual freedoms and to social trust.

In the final part of this book, the author focuses on the European legal framework of privacy, data protection, freedom of expression, tort law and AI specific regulation and explores how these frameworks perform in relation to the challenges that have been identified. Based on this analysis, the author is able to highlight the gaps in the regulatory framework that need to be addressed in order to secure our society against the negative effects of generative AI, and to examine the choices the regulatory body has in trying to address these challenges. It turns out that the answers are not straightforward.

Ebook editions for this book are available under an Open Access licence, which is CC BY-NC-ND 4.0. The dedicated URL for this book in your library and other outlets may be hidden. However, you can find it within your library any time you search for its title.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
